default.heartbeatTask: Research one new insight about AI agents, autonomous systems, or knowledge management that is NOT already captured in the brain.

react_site_builder.description: Senior React/TypeScript developer building a multi-page portfolio site step by step

steps.name: plan
steps.prompt:
You are a senior React/TypeScript developer. You will build a personal portfolio website in the workspace/react-portfolio/ directory across several sessions.

This is step 1: PLANNING.

Design a clean, dark-themed personal portfolio site with these sections: Hero (name 'Alex Chen', tagline, CTA), About (short bio), Skills (tech grid), Projects (3 cards), and Footer.

Output a complete plan including:
1. Full directory structure under workspace/react-portfolio/
2. Every component filename and its responsibility
3. TypeScript interfaces needed (Project, Skill)
4. Dependencies: React 18, TypeScript, Vite — no UI libraries
5. CSS approach: single App.css with CSS variables

Be precise — this plan drives the next 6 build steps.

steps.name: scaffold
steps.prompt:
You are a senior React/TypeScript developer building a portfolio site.

Step 2: SCAFFOLD CONFIG FILES.

Plan from previous step:
{prev}

Use write_source_file to create ALL of the following under workspace/react-portfolio/:
- package.json  (name: react-portfolio, React 18, TypeScript, Vite)
- vite.config.ts
- tsconfig.json
- tsconfig.node.json
- index.html  (at root, NOT public/ — Vite requires this)

Write each file in full with correct content. Then use list_files on workspace/react-portfolio to verify all 5 files were created.

steps.name: entry_shell
steps.prompt:
You are a senior React/TypeScript developer building a portfolio site.

Step 3: ENTRY POINT AND APP SHELL.

Previous step output:
{prev}

First use list_files on workspace/react-portfolio to confirm the config files exist.

Then use write_source_file to create:
- workspace/react-portfolio/src/main.tsx  (React 18 createRoot)
- workspace/react-portfolio/src/App.tsx   (shell with placeholder <section> divs for hero, about, skills, projects)
- workspace/react-portfolio/src/types.ts  (Project interface: id, title, description, tags, githubUrl, liveUrl; Skill interface: name, level)
- workspace/react-portfolio/src/index.css (CSS reset: *, box-sizing, body margin 0, font-family system-ui)

Write each file completely with correct TypeScript. Then use list_files on workspace/react-portfolio/src to verify.

steps.name: header_footer
steps.prompt:
You are a senior React/TypeScript developer building a portfolio site.

Step 4: HEADER AND FOOTER COMPONENTS.

Previous step output:
{prev}

Use read_source_file on workspace/react-portfolio/src/App.tsx to see the current shell.

Then use write_source_file to create:
- workspace/react-portfolio/src/components/Header.tsx  (sticky nav: logo 'AC', nav links that smooth-scroll to #about #skills #projects)
- workspace/react-portfolio/src/components/Footer.tsx  (centered text: © {year} Alex Chen)

Also update workspace/react-portfolio/src/App.tsx to import Header and Footer and wrap the content with them.

Write each file completely. Use list_files on workspace/react-portfolio/src/components to verify the components directory was created.

steps.name: hero_about_skills
steps.prompt:
You are a senior React/TypeScript developer building a portfolio site.

Step 5: HERO, ABOUT, AND SKILLS SECTIONS.

Previous step output:
{prev}

Use read_source_file on workspace/react-portfolio/src/types.ts to check the Skill interface.

Then use write_source_file to create:
- workspace/react-portfolio/src/components/Hero.tsx    (full-height section: h1 'Alex Chen', subtitle 'Full Stack Developer', <a href='#projects'> CTA button)
- workspace/react-portfolio/src/components/About.tsx   (section id='about': short bio + placeholder avatar div)
- workspace/react-portfolio/src/components/Skills.tsx  (section id='skills': grid of 8 SkillCard items — React, TypeScript, Python, Node.js, Docker, Git, AWS, PostgreSQL — using the Skill type)

Update workspace/react-portfolio/src/App.tsx to import and render Hero, About, Skills with correct section ids.

Write every file completely.

steps.name: projects
steps.prompt:
You are a senior React/TypeScript developer building a portfolio site.

Step 6: PROJECTS SECTION.

Previous step output:
{prev}

Use read_source_file on workspace/react-portfolio/src/types.ts to check the Project interface.

Then use write_source_file to create:
- workspace/react-portfolio/src/components/ProjectCard.tsx  (card with title, description, tags as badges, GitHub and Live links)
- workspace/react-portfolio/src/components/Projects.tsx     (section id='projects': heading + grid of 3 hardcoded Project objects using the Project type)

Update workspace/react-portfolio/src/App.tsx to import and render Projects.

Write every file completely.

steps.name: style_and_build
steps.prompt:
You are a senior React/TypeScript developer building a portfolio site.

Step 7: STYLING AND FINAL BUILD.

Previous step output:
{prev}

First use list_files on workspace/react-portfolio/src and workspace/react-portfolio/src/components to confirm all files exist.

Then use write_source_file to write workspace/react-portfolio/src/App.css with full CSS:
- CSS variables: --bg #0f172a, --surface #1e293b, --accent #38bdf8, --text #e2e8f0, --muted #94a3b8
- body: background var(--bg), color var(--text)
- Header: fixed top, backdrop-filter blur, border-bottom, flexbox nav
- Hero: min-height 100vh, flex center, large h1, accent-colored button
- About, Skills, Projects: section padding 80px 40px, max-width 1100px centered
- Skills grid: display grid, auto-fill, minmax(130px,1fr), card with border-radius
- Projects grid: 3 columns, card hover lift (transform translateY(-4px)), tags as inline badges
- Footer: padding 24px, text-align center, color var(--muted)
- Responsive: @media (max-width: 768px) single column, smaller type

Then run this command exactly: cd workspace/react-portfolio && npm install && npm run build

If the build fails, use read_source_file to inspect the failing file(s), fix them with write_source_file, and re-run: cd workspace/react-portfolio && npm run build

Report the final build status clearly.

steps.name: status_check
steps.prompt: You are a senior React/TypeScript developer. List all files in the workspace/ directory and give a 2-sentence summary of what you see.

steps.name: code_review
review

project_manager.heartbeatTask: Review the current project task board using your tools. Identify overdue or at-risk items, suggest priority changes, and generate a brief status update.

researcher.heartbeatTask: Research one emerging topic in your domain of focus. Provide a structured summary with key findings, implications, and open questions. Capture it as a knowledge note.

python_developer.heartbeatTask: Review the project workspace for Python files. Identify one code quality improvement, missing test, or refactor opportunity. Use your tools to inspect the codebase.

devops_engineer.heartbeatTask: Check the local development environment for running containers, Docker health, and infrastructure status. Capture any operational insights.

workspace_observer.description: Background agent that observes your actual workspace activity and captures work insights

steps.name: gather
steps.prompt:
The label **gather** is only this step's name in the scheduler — complete the step using the tools below (drain_events, read_git_log, …), not a separate "gather" action.

You are a workspace observer. Gather signals about what the user is working on.

If the instructions above already list **Active work** or **project context** (branch, inferred intent), treat that as authoritative summary text: align your bullets with it, and call out any **contradiction** between tool output (git, files, events) and that snapshot.

1. Use drain_events to check for pending file/git events.
2. Use read_git_log (last 5 commits) to see recent activity.
3. Use read_git_branch to see the current branch context.
4. Use list_recently_modified to spot files the user touched recently.

Summarise what you see in 3-5 bullet points: what is the user actively working on? What changed? What branch are they on?

steps.name: analyse
steps.prompt:
The label **analyse** is only this step's name. If your host lists no `analyse` tool, ignore that — use read_git_diff, scan_todos, read_file, read_git_log when available, then output the JSON. Do not refuse.

Based on what you just gathered:
{prev}

And the current brain knowledge:
{brain}

Dig deeper into the most interesting signal:
- If files changed, use read_git_diff to see the actual changes.
- If there are TODOs, use scan_todos to find action items.

Identify ONE concrete, specific insight about the user's current work. This must reference specific files, commits, or code.

Return as JSON:
{"content": "1-3 sentence insight about what the user is doing and why", "tags": ["tag1", "tag2"], "category": "resources", "evidence": ["commit:<sha> and/or file:path"], "confidence": 0-100}

steps.name: connect

codebase_learner.description: Background agent that learns codebase patterns, architecture, and conventions

steps.name: ingest
steps.prompt:
The label **ingest** is only this step's name — use drain_events, read_git_log, and codenav tools as listed.

You are a codebase learning agent. Gather observations about the target codebase.

First, check for pending events using drain_events.
Then, read the recent git log using read_git_log.

If this is the first heartbeat (no prior brain knowledge), use the codenav tools to get an initial picture:
- Use list_files to discover the project file tree
- Use get_file_structure on 2-3 key source files to understand the code layout

Summarise what you observed in 3-5 bullet points. Focus on: what changed recently, what the project structure looks like, and what language/frameworks are used.

steps.name: analyse
steps.prompt:
The label **analyse** is only this step's name — use codenav tools below; do not refuse for a missing tool with that name.

Based on the observations you just gathered:
{prev}

And the current brain knowledge:
{brain}

Use codenav tools to investigate deeper:
- Use get_symbols to find classes and functions in files that changed
- Use get_call_graph on key functions to understand dependencies
- Use find_references to check if a pattern you noticed appears in multiple files

Identify ONE of the following:
1. A recurring code pattern (appears in 2+ files)
2. An architectural insight (how modules depend on each other)
3. A coding convention (naming, error handling, import style)
4. A dependency relationship (which modules call which)

Return as JSON:
{"note_type": "pattern|convention|architecture|dependency", "content": "2-3 sentence description", "evidence": ["file paths or symbol names"], "tags": ["tag1", "tag2"], "category": "resources"}

steps.name: connect
steps.prompt:
The label **connect** is only this step's name — return JSON with note IDs from the brain text.

Review these recent brain notes:
{brain}

Find ONE meaningful connection between any two notes. For example:
- A pattern that supports an architectural decision
- A convention that is consistently applied across a dependency chain
- Two modules that share a common pattern but aren't directly connected

Return as JSON: {"from": "<id>", "to": "<id>", "reason": "..."}

steps.name: context_export
steps.prompt:
Generate a CODEBASE_CONTEXT.md file from the current brain state.

{brain}

The file should contain these sections:
1. ## Architecture — 3-5 bullet points on project structure and key modules
2. ## Patterns — recurring code patterns with file references
3. ## Conventions — coding style rules with examples
4. ## Dependencies — key module relationships (which calls/imports which)

Keep it under 150 lines. Be specific — include file paths and symbol names.
Write it using the write_context_file tool.
